coredata.dc:title: Paper-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensors for on-site sensing of sulfide based on the target-induced inner filter effect
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Yao
Ye, Simin
Tian, Jinxiao
Leng, Anqin
Deng, Yurong
Zhang, Jinyi
Zheng, Chengbin
coredata.dc:description: Dissolved sulfide tends to species transformation and loss upon leaving the matrix, thus the development of a practical on-site determination of sulfide is crucial for environmental monitoring and human health. In this work, a novel paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for the field analysis of sulfide, which system was constructed by the inner filter effect (IFE) of CdS quantum dots (QDs) toward carbon dots (C-dots). Instead of an aqueous phase system, the conversion of sulfide to its hydride would induce the in-situ formation of CdS QDs on the paper, which acted as an energy acceptor to quench the emission of C-dots, leading to a variation of ratiometric fluorescence from blue to yellow with the increasing concentration of sulfide. Moreover, we proposed a smartphone-based fluorescence capture device integrated with a programmed Python program, accomplishing both color recognition and accurate detection of sulfide. Under the optimal condition, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor allowed for the on-site analysis of sulfide with a limit of detection of 0.05 μM. The accuracy of the sensor was validated via the successful field analysis of environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries. Compared to other fluorescence methods used for sulfide analysis, this developed system retains the advantages of label-free, low-cost, ease of operation, and miniaturization, showing great potential for the measurement of sulfide on-site, as well as environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfide analysis
Ratiometric fluorescent
Inner filter effect
On-site
Paper

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical procedure of paper-based ratiometric fluorescent for sulfide analysis
Fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent capture device
IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence from in situ synthesis of CdS QDs and C-dots
Construction of a portable device for sulfide quantification
Analytical performance of IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sulfided analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22206135 and 22104101 ) for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence emission spectra of obtained CdS QDs in an aqueous phase, inserted: the fluorescence images. (B) TEM characteristic of obtained CdS QDs. (C) The fluorescence intensity of CdS QDs in the presence of different concentrations of sulfide. (D) The fluorescence emission spectra of CdS QDs on paper, inserted: the fluorescence images.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CdS QDs and fluorescence spectra of C-dots. (B) Fluorescence emission of C-dots and the presence of Cd2+ and S2-. (C) Fluorescence decay of C-dots with the generated CdS QDs (Ex: 370 nm, Em: 450 nm). (C-dots, 5 mg/L; Cd2+, 1 mM; GSH, 1 mM; S2-, 1 mM, pH 6.5).

(A) The detailed sketch and overall design of the developed fluorescence readout device. (B) The detailed angle between installed excitation LED and emission capture. (C) Physical photograph of the whole device with different perspectives.

(A) Fluorescence photograph of this IFE-based ratiometric sensor upon the addition of various concentrations of S2- under optimal reaction conditions. (B) Linear relationship of (R+G+B/B value) versus the S2-. (C) Ion coexistence of the sensor for the analysis of S2-.
Schematic diagram of the whole workflow for the analysis of sulfide on-site using the developed platform.

Schematic diagram of the proposed IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence for the quantitative sensing of sulfide based on the developed portable device integrated heating system, fluorescence capture, and readout.
Analytical results in CRMs by this proposed strategy.
The tested and recovery of sulfide analysis in water samples in the field based on the proposed IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence using the proposed portable device.

head.ce:title: Paper-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensors for on-site sensing of sulfide based on the target-induced inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China School of Basic Medical Sciences & Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Green Chemistry & Technology of MOE, College of Chemistry, Sichuan University, Chengdu, Sichuan 610064, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sichuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Sichuan Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Chengdu, Sichuan 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dissolved sulfide tends to species transformation and loss upon leaving the matrix, thus the development of a practical on-site determination of sulfide is crucial for environmental monitoring and human health. In this work, a novel paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was developed for the field analysis of sulfide, which system was constructed by the inner filter effect (IFE) of CdS quantum dots (QDs) toward carbon dots (C-dots). Instead of an aqueous phase system, the conversion of sulfide to its hydride would induce the in-situ formation of CdS QDs on the paper, which acted as an energy acceptor to quench the emission of C-dots, leading to a variation of ratiometric fluorescence from blue to yellow with the increasing concentration of sulfide. Moreover, we proposed a smartphone-based fluorescence capture device integrated with a programmed Python program, accomplishing both color recognition and accurate detection of sulfide. Under the optimal condition, this ratiometric fluorescence sensor allowed for the on-site analysis of sulfide with a limit of detection of 0.05 μM. The accuracy of the sensor was validated via the successful field analysis of environmental water samples with satisfactory recoveries. Compared to other fluorescence methods used for sulfide analysis, this developed system retains the advantages of label-free, low-cost, ease of operation, and miniaturization, showing great potential for the measurement of sulfide on-site, as well as environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dissolved sulfide widely exists in natural and wastewater released from households and is abundant in sulfur-related industries, which is also formed in biological system activities, such as microbial reduction of sulfate and bacterial degradation of sulfur-containing substances [1–4]. It is estimated that these activities provided a certain amount of dissolved sulfide, which was easily converted by the living system into harmful hydrogen sulfide, causing serious harm to the environment and humans, even at low concentrations [5–8]. Owing to the characteristic of high reactivity, environmental dissolved sulfide tends to be consumed by heavy metal ions and dissolved oxygen during sample storage and transportation, inevitably resulting in loss of sulfide and unreliable detection using laboratory instruments [9]. Therefore, it is indispensable to develop a sensitive, accurate, and portable method for the analysis of sulfide [10–12].

To date, the determination of sulfide mainly depends on laboratory-based approaches, including electrochemical [13,14], spectroscopy [15–17], and chromatography techniques [18,19], which typically suffer from time-consuming, complicated sample preparation, bulk instrumentation, and low sensitivity. With the increasing pressure on the environmental monitoring of pollutants at a low level, a powerful on-site method with the advantages of being cost-effective, simple, and sensitive has become of great interest to environmental analysts [20,21]. In the recent decade, fluorimetric-based techniques have emerged as alternative candidates for the field analysis of sulfide based on the specific interaction between sulfide and established fluorescent probes [22–24]. However, conventional fluorometry usually involves the measurement of one fluorescence signal, which sensitivity would be easily susceptible to the background and environmental factors [25,26]. Through recording the ratio of fluorescent intensities at two different emitters, ratiometric fluorescence has been widely established for the analysis of hydrogen sulfide, especially in vivo [27–29], while its application in environmental sulfide detection was still limited. Ratiometric fluorescence could achieve the target analysis by recording the ratio of the fluorescence intensity, thus achieving more accurate, sensitive, and effective detection performance with high tolerance to environmental disturbance. In particular, the inner filter effect (IFE) as a non-irradiation energy conversion model originated from the absorption of the excitation and/or emission light by the absorber, which induced ratiometric fluorescence sensing has the superiority in simplicity and flexibility because it’s not involved in any fluorophore modification and the link of absorber with fluorophore [30–32]. However, most of the available fluorescence methods are operated in the aqueous phase and to a certain suffer from matrix interference in practical environmental samples such as color, turbidity, and impaired applicability [33]. Although it is a prevailing trend that the fluorescence sensing system integrated with a portable device enables the real-time analysis of different targets [34,35], it remains a challenge for the field analysis of sulfide.

Herein, a paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully developed for the field detection of sulfide integrating fluorescence IFE to a portable smartphone-based readout system. Instead of aqueous phase sensing, the sulfide analyte was first converted to its hydride and then reacted with Cd2+ and glutathione (GSH) coated on paper and in situ instantly formed cadmium sulfide quantum dots (CdS QDs), which combined with the carbon dots (C-dots) on the paper to construct a ratiometric fluorescence using IFE principle ( Scheme 1). The use of vapor generation as a sample introduction can significantly separate the analyte from the environment aqueous sample and avoid matrix interference. Moreover, the specific reaction between Cd2+ and S2- also enables the sensing selectivity. To integrate the sensing process and quantitation of the fluorescence signals from the paper-based sensor, we further fabricated a portable device integrating vapor generation and fluorescence capture modules by 3D-printing technology. Meanwhile, a programmed Python program was used to accomplish the on-site fluorescence readout of sulfide. The proposed paper-based sulfide sensing device is successfully applied to detect sulfide in practical water samples with good recoveries. The integration of the sensing and readout process greatly enhances the practicability in actual field environment monitoring.

The ink cartridge used was first modified before printing. An unopened ink cartridge was taken and saw out a hole about 1 cm on the side to contain the printed solution, which was afterward rinsed using high-purity (18.2 MΩ cm) deionized water (DIW) several times until clear and dried in an oven at 70 °C for 3 h. Then, 1.3 mL of 1.0 mol/L CdCl2, 1.3 mL of 0.1 mol/L GSH, 40 μL of 0.8 g C-dots, and Tris-HCl buffer solution were prepared to compose the “ink” and injected into the inkjet cartridge. Eventually, the paper evenly spread with Cd2+/GSH/C-dots was printed, which was further cut to a round shape to adapt the headspace cap.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical procedure of paper-based ratiometric fluorescent for sulfide analysis
ce:section.ce:para: A series concentration of S2- were added to the headspace bottle, which was then sealed with a cap decorated with Cd2+/GSH/C-dots-printed paper. A 0.5 mL of 15% H2SO4 was injected into the bottle and then transfer to the integrated water bath device under 50 °C for 20 min. The produced H2S from a liquid phase to the headspace and reacted with Cd2+/GSH to result in the synthesis of CdS QDs on the paper. Subsequently, the paper was taken out using a tweezer and placed into the developed fluorescence image capture device. The RGB acquirement of paper could be extracted and their eventual concentration details also exported with the aid of developing a Python program on a smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ratiometric fluorescent capture device
ce:section.ce:para: The plastic holder (152 mm × 80 mm × 75 mm) was designed and printed by a UV curing 3D printer (Lite 600, Union Tech, China) with photosensitive resin. The aluminum alloy metal bath with three grooves was customized using a CNC machine. Two 3.7-V (3200 mAh) lithium battery pack (15 mm × 30 mm × 70 mm) was supplied to the thermal unit (operating at 50 °C, maximum power: 3 W for ∼4 h) and its temperature was regulated through the measurement of a thermistor. A LED (365 nm, 3.6 V, 700 mA) was embedded in the top of the device, which was also powered by the lithium battery pack. A concave groove was designed to load paper perpendicular to the place of the smartphone (OPPO, RENO Pro) to capture fluorescence images. The eventual data output was accomplished by the programmed Python program, by which red, green, and blue values of four image zones were acquired and processed.

Four field water samples obtained from tap water, campus pond, Funan river, and Dong lake (Chengdu, Sichuan) were selected for the site analysis. The test paper was printed in advance and fixed in the bottle cap. 9.5 mL of the collected samples were first filtered and immediately injected into the reaction bottles, together with 0.5 mL of 15% H2SO4 for the reaction. The bottles was then placed in the heating module of the proposed device for the water bath and followed by the transfer to the fluorescence capture sector and final concentration output.

Attributed to excellent fluorescence, optical properties, and facile synthesis method, sulfide-mediated formation of CdS QDs has been developed and widely used in biosensor applications [36–38]. While sulfide-induced the instant synthesis of CdS QDs and thus achieved the detection of sulfide has not been reported. We herein first explored the feasibility of the synthesis of CdS QDs through the simple mixture of cadmium, sulfide source, and GSH in an aqueous phase under room temperature. From Fig. 1A, it can be instantly observed a distinct emission peak at 550 nm and a corresponding yellow fluorescence image, which morphology and size were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and shown in Fig. 1B. The CdS QDs are uniform with an average diameter of about 5 nm and the component of Cd and S was also demonstrated by energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) (Fig. S1). It suggests that a simple mixing method of source indeed achieves the instant synthesis of QDs with excellent fluorescence performance. In addition, the relationship of fluorescence intensity of obtained CdS QDs between the concentrations of sulfide was further investigated. As shown in Fig. 1C, the fluorescence emission was significantly increased with increasing the concentration of sulfide, indicating the feasibility of the used CdS QDs fluorescence for quantitative analysis of sulfide. Therefore, the feasibility of sulfide-induced instant formation of CdS QDs in a gaseous phase was studied. A paper printed with Cd2+/GSH was adopted to be a cap and placed in a headspace bottle, which was reacted with volatile sulfide generated by the addition of H2SO4 into an S2- solution. From Fig. 1D, it can be observed that the paper displayed wider fluorescence spectra compared with the obtained CdS QDs in the aqueous phase shown in Fig. 1A, which is probably the uneven distribution of particle size in a paper. While the obtained CdS QDs synthesized in both liquid and paper matrix exhibited a 550 nm characteristic emission, indicating the feasibility of the in situ formation of CdS QDs in a paper matrix with the mediated of the volatile sulfide. Instead of commonly sodium sulfide or thiourea in an aqueous phase, the sulfide analyte was converted to H2S prior to its instant reaction with Cd2+ and GSH coated on paper and the in situ formation of CdS QDs, which is consistent with the available precipitation [39,40]. H2S-induced synthesis of CdS QDs offers the advantage of using a gaseous reducing agent, which can facilitate faster reaction kinetics and achieve the instant formation of CdS QDs. The selectivity of this strategy to sulfide benefits from the high affinity between Cd2+ and S2- and the specific formation of CdS QDs. Additionally, only sulfide could produce its hydride under acidic conditions and the homologous elements of sulfur, such as Se and Te, can only form their hydrides in the presence of tetrahydroborate, which allows the specific fluorescence variation owing to the presence of sulfide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence from in situ synthesis of CdS QDs and C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: After the verification of volatile sulfide-induced instant formation of CdS QDs and its fluorescence intensity was in direct proportion to S2- concentration, a ratiometric fluorescence with a much more noticeable visualization through a selection of fluorescent nanomaterial that excitation and emission spectra can overlap with the absorption spectra of CdS QDs. C-dots, as an excellent fluorescent nanomaterial characteristic with a regulable emission, has become a good candidate as an internal standard applied in ratiometric fluorescence sensor [41–43]. Therefore, C-dots with an optimal excitation and emission wavelength at 350 and 450 nm were synthesized ( Fig. 2A), which perfectly fit the absorption spectra of CdS QDs and applied as an internal standard. The detailed preparation and characteristic of C-dots are shown in Section 3 in SI and Fig. S2. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor was further constructed with CdS QDs and C-dots, which were mixed with Cd2+/GSH and C-dots. As shown in Fig. 2B, C-dot together with Cd2+ exhibited the featured emission at 450 nm, with the addition of S2-, its fluorescence intensity significantly decreased and the formation emission at around 550 nm attributed to the feature peak of CdS QDs, suggesting that the successful formation of CdS QDs, which served as both absorber and emitter. Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor could be achieved for the analysis of S2-. To further explore the mechanism of sulfide-induced formation of CdS QDs, the fluorescence lifetimes of C-dots together with or without adding sulfide were studied. As shown in Fig. 2C, the fluorescence lifetimes of C-dots was reduced from 9 ns to 7 ns with the formation of CdS QDs, indicating the sulfide-induced fluorescence quenching of C-dots may involve fluorescence resonance energy transfer. However, the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots (Fig. 2B) was reduced to a greater extent than the fluorescence lifetime, indicating the quenching mechanism was dominated by IFE.

As an essential component, ligand plays a role in the synthesis of CdS QDs and three kinds of ligand with negative charge were selected to explore their effect on the formation. From Fig. S3, BSA exhibited a relatively weaker fluorescence intensity, while the intensity signal by GSH was higher than that obtained by L-Cys, thus GSH was selected as the ligand in this developed ratiometric sensor. The formation of QDs involved the electrostatic adsorption between Cd2+ and ligand and thus the role of solution pH was explored. As shown in Fig. S4A, the optimal fluorescence intensity obtained in a basic condition was generally higher than that obtained in acid, because the negative condition would be beneficial for electrostatic adsorption and promote the formation of CdS QDs. While the obtained emission wavelengths were gradually blue shift with the pH increasing from 5.5 to 9.5 (Fig. S4B), indicating that the pH is closely related to the emission intensity and wavelength of CdS QDs. Considering a wide emission gap between CdS QDs and C-dots would be conducive to IFE, therefore, pH 6.5 was chosen for the reaction condition.

It has been theoretically demonstrated that the reduced diameter of the headspace area would significantly increase the density of forming particles and the thickness of the absorption layer, thus inducing improve the sensitivity for the vapor-induced fluorescence variation on a headspace paper [44]. In this work, we found that the fluorescence shown on the paper might interfere with water vapor when the diameter was smaller than 8 mm, therefore, 8 mm was chosen as the headspace diameter. Considering the gradual quenching of blue fluorescence with increasing concentration of S2-, different signal outputs including R/B, G/B, (R+G)/B, (G+B)/B, (R+B)/B, and (R+G+B)/B values were first studied, respectively. As shown in Fig. S5, both acquired ordinates for (R+G)/B and (R+G+B)/B values exhibited a better linear relationship (R2 =0.999) with the detected sulfide concentration (0.4–5 µM). By contrast, the obtained (R+G+B)/B value was greater than (R+G)/B, which would be conducive to improving the sensitivity of this method for the analysis of sulfide. Therefore, the ratio of (R+G+B)/B value was chosen as the signal output. The sulfide-induced formation of CdS QDs attributed to the volatility of generated H2S once the addition of H2SO4, thus, the reaction temperature and time play role in the determination of S2-. As shown in Fig. S6A, the obtained signal was dramatically increased in the temperature range between 30 and 50 °C and then reached a plateau beyond 50 °C. It could be attributed to the increased temperature would decrease the solubility of sulfide and accelerate the volatility of H2S, which would be beneficial to the formation of CdS QDs and improve the sensitivity for sulfide analysis [44]. Besides, the reaction time of H2SO4 and sulfide affected the formation of CdS QDs and induced eventual ratiometric sensor variation. The fluorescence signal remarkably increased over time and reached a plateau at 20 min (Fig. S6B). Therefore, 50 °C of reaction temperature and 20 min were chosen as optimal conditions for the analysis of sulfide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a portable device for sulfide quantification
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed sketch and overall design of the fluorescence readout device are shown in Fig. 3A. The device consists of three seamless modules, including a heating module for volatile sulfide generation, an optical module for excitation, and a smartphone-installed Python program for image capture and data readout. The heating module was designed for controlling the temperature at 50 °C to promote the generation of H2S, which is customized by a ceramic plate and aluminum alloy metal to form three grooves matched headspace bottle. The temperature was regulated through the measurement of a thermistor embedded in the thermal block. Considering that the temperature was acquired from the heating block instead of the analyte solution on the bottle, we further explored the temperature profile over time. From Fig. S7, it can be observed that the heating block rocked around 55 °C within 10 min and then maintained the average temperature at 53 °C for 20 min, which is efficient to maintain the volatile sulfide reaction process. The optical unit incorporates a LED excitation source (365 nm) arranged to be perpendicular to the top box, and a concave groove was adaptive to paper loading, which was designed at a certain angle to minimize the excitation interference on the fluorescence image (Fig. 3B). The power of both the LED unit and the heating system was supplied by two rechargeable lithium batteries. Placed on the top side of the box, a smartphone installed a programmed Python program was developed to image capture and data output. This program could provide instructions including image capture, data analysis, and test result using mobile phone. The smartphone camera could automatically block UV light, thus emission filter was not acquired in this system. The real photograph of the whole device with different perspectives as exhibited in Fig. 3C and the overall block diagram design of the device were illustrated and shown in Section 10 (Figs. S8–S11). It’s noted that the whole cost of the designed device is around $25.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of IFE-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for sulfided analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized conditions, the performance of the detection of S2- by this proposed ratiometric fluorescence sensor was further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4A, the blue fluorescence image of the sensor visibly weakened with the addition of 0.2 μM of S2-, and gradually quenched to light blue when the S2- was increased to 3.0 μM. With an increase in S2- concentration to 5.0 μM, the blue fluorescence disappeared and exhibited light gray, eventually from noticeable dark yellow (8.0 μM of sulfide) to luminous yellow (12.0 μM of sulfide). Combined with a programmed Python, the designed portable readout device acquired (R+G+B)/B value and sulfide concentration (from 0.2 to 8.0 μM) exhibited a good linear relationship in Fig. 4B, and the linear fitting equation was y = 0.286x + 1.34 (R2 =0.998). Based on the definition of three times the standard deviation of the blank signal [45], the calculated limits of detection (LODs) was 0.05 μM. Therefore, the sulfide can be visualized from blue to yellow by the naked eye at a concentration as low as 0.2 μM, while a better sensitivity can be achieved by this developed portable device integrated programmed Python program. It has been suggested by Chinese National and WHO International Standards that the allowable sulfide in drinking water should not exceed 0.6 μM and 1.5 μM, respectively, which are both in the detection range of this proposed method. To assess the precision and repeatability of analytical results, six duplicate samples were measured under the same conditions and the obtained relative standard deviation was 3.2%.

The LOD of the proposed ratiometric sensor was compared with the available methods for sulfide detection (spectrophotometry, colorimetry, electrochemistry, etc.), and their analytical performances were summarized in Table S1. It can conclude that this developed sensor shows excellent or comparable performance for the analysis of sulfide, in which LOD is lower than most of the non-fluorescent strategies. It’s noted that all the available fluorescence methods were operated in an aqueous phase, which to a certain exposure to the fluorescence interference from the colored or turbid samples, thus limiting their applicability. Therefore, based on the use of vapor generation as a sample introduction means, this sensor is simple and greatly reduced matrix interference. Moreover, the available methods depend on laboratory spectrometers and their field analysis of sulfide is still limited.

To study the selectivity of the proposed strategy for sulfide detection, possible coexisting ions in the actual water sample, including K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, SO3 2-, PO4 3-, CO3 2-, and NO3 - were examined together with sulfide by recording their fluorescence intensity in the ratiometric system. Fig. 4C displays that all tested ions almost did not affect the obtained fluorescence signal compared with the presence of sulfide even at their 100-fold concentration of sulfide, suggesting this sensor presents excellent selectivity to distinguish sulfide from common ions in actual environmental water samples. The stability of the prepared paper was also explored and the results are shown in Fig. S12. The paper printed with Cd2+/GSH/C-dots was stored at 4 °C and kept in a dark place for 0, 1, 2, 4, 6, and 8 days. The results show that the obtained fluorescence images change little over time, suggesting that this proposed fluorescence sensor exhibited good preservation performance, which would be in favor of its on-site application for the analysis of sulfide.

The precision of the developed sensor was first validated by analyzing two sample CRMs and the analytical results are summarized in Table 1. A t-test results exhibit that the obtained analytical results were consistent with the certified values at the confidence level of 95%, demonstrating the accuracy of the proposed ratiometric sensor on the determination of sulfide. To assess the practical application of the developed sensor combined with the portable device in the field determination of sulfide, four environmental samples collected from tap water, campus pond, river, and lake were tested after simple filtration, which the on-site analysis photograph is shown in Fig. 5. From Table 2, there were unsurprising that no sulfide was detected in tap water, and the campus pond and Funan River also can not be detected. In contrast, the sulfide in Dong Lake was detected as 0.40 μM, under the threshold value (0.60 μM) set by Chinese National Standards. To further confirm the applicability of the proposed method free of colored matrix interference, two kinds of colored dye (resazurin and methyl violet) were added to simulate actual colored water samples (Fig. S13). The obtained results showed that recoveries ranging from 88% to 112% were obtained for all the samples spiked with 0.50 and 2.00 μM of sulfide, confirming the practicability of the proposed sensor on the quantification of sulfide in colored aqueous samples.

This work presents a simple method and a portable device for the on-site analysis of sulfide based on a paper-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which is achieved by the IFE between C-dots and CdS QDs in situ formed in the presence of sulfide and induced fluorescence variation from blue to yellow. Instead of a conventional aqueous phase system, the sulfide was converted to its hydride and instantly induced the formation of CdS QDs on the paper, which could significantly separate the analyte from the condensed phase and avoid matrix interference. Importantly, a homemade portable fluorescence capture device integrated a designed Python program to realize the automated quantification based on the sulfide concentration-dependent ratiometric fluorescent images. In this way, a large instrument, as well as a sample collection process were not required, greatly simplifying the analytical process and avoiding the conversion of sulfide. Eventually, this strategy can be applied in the determination of sulfide in a real water sample with satisfactory results, and we believe that this proposed sensor combined with a low-cost fluorescence readout device can serve as a valuable tool for sulfide monitoring.

Yao Lin: Methodology, Validation, Data curation, Investigation, Writing. Simin Ye: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation. Jinxiao Tian: Methodology. Anqin Leng: Investigation. Yurong Deng: Writing – review & editing. Jinyi Zhang: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Chengbin Zheng: Project administration, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22206135 and 22104101) for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: The manuscript was written through the contributions of all authors. All authors have approved the final version of the manuscript.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2023.132201.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material.
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Animal evolution, bioturbation, and the sulfate concentration of the oceans
Proc Natl Acad Sci USA
